perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall carefully restrict access to Confidential Information to any employees, contractors, agents, and any other third parties as is reasonably required and shall require those persons to sign non-disclosure restrictions at least as protective as those in this Agreement. Receiving Party shall not, without prior written approval of Disclosing Party, use for Receiving Party's own benefit, publish, copy, transmit, use, or otherwise disclose to others, or permit the use by others for their benefit or to the detriment of Disclosing Party, any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall restrict access to Confidential Information to employees and contractors. Receiving Party shall not, without prior written approval of Disclosing Party, use for Receiving Party's own benefit, publish, copy, transmit, use, or otherwise disclose to others, or permit the use by others for their benefit or to the detriment of Disclosing Party, any Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'carefully' and deleting the obligation to extend the confidentiality to agents and other third parties, this clause weakens the protective measures for the disclosing party's confidential information. Omitting the requirement for downstream non-disclosure agreements introduces legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1 because it reduces the reasonable measures required to protect trade secrets. By not requiring non-disclosure agreements with agents and other relevant third parties with access to Confidential Information, the receiving party fails to take adequate steps to prevent misappropriation, potentially losing trade secret protection under California law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the protection of confidential information by removing the 'carefully' qualifier and omitting the requirement for non-disclosure agreements with agents and other third parties. The Law Explanation argues this contradicts Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1, because it reduces the reasonable measures required to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' By not requiring agents and third parties to sign non-disclosure agreements, the Receiving Party increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure, thus undermining the effort to maintain secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: Unless a business agreement, contract, or other business arrangement is entered into, each Party agrees to return or destroy, with written confirmation, all Confidential Information in any form upon conclusion of the evaluation of entering into the transaction or other business arrangement or upon the specific request of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless a business agreement, contract, or other business arrangement is entered into, each Party agrees to return or destroy all Confidential Information in any form upon conclusion of the evaluation of entering into the transaction or other business arrangement or upon the specific request of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'with written confirmation' removes the documented proof of destruction or return of confidential information. This weakens the disclosing party's ability to enforce the agreement and verify compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly about NDAs, this modification weakens the enforceability of the NDA in a California court. Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 2031.010 concerns discovery processes, and written confirmation of the destruction of data helps prove compliance in court. By not having this evidence, the disclosing party's ability to obtain the evidence to enforce this agreement is weaker, and the other party can claim data was destroyed and the disclosing party can't disprove that claim.

MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 14. Inspection, Copying, Testing, Sampling, and Production of Documents, Electronically Stored Information, Tangible Things, Land, and Other Property [2031.010 - 2031.510] ( Heading of Chapter 14 amended by Stats. 2012, Ch. 72, Sec. 30. ) ARTICLE 1. Inspection Demand [2031.010 - 2031.060] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2031.010. (a) Any party may obtain discovery within the scope delimited by Chapter 2 (commencing with Section 2017.010), and subject to the restrictions set forth in Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 2019.010), by inspecting, copying, testing, or sampling documents, tangible things, land or other property, and electronically stored information in the possession, custody, or control of any other party to the action. (b) A party may demand that any other party produce and permit the party making the demand, or someone acting on the demanding party’s behalf, to inspect and to copy a document that is in the possession, custody, or control of the party on whom the demand is made. (c) A party may demand tha
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'with written confirmation' directly undermines the ability to verify compliance with the agreement, potentially conflicting with legal requirements for discovery. The provided snippet, Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 2031.010, highlights the importance of producing documents for inspection and copying during discovery. Removing the written confirmation weakens the disclosing party's ability to prove compliance if the other party claims data was destroyed. Thus, the modified text diminishes the enforceability of the original agreement, creating a legal loophole related to data destruction verification.

perturbation.original_text: All matters affecting the interpretation of this Agreement and the rights of the parties hereto shall be governed by the laws of the United States and the State of California, without reference to rules governing choice of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: All matters affecting the interpretation of this Agreement and the rights of the parties hereto shall be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'the laws of the United States,' the contract now only references state law. This could remove federal protections. It introduces legal risk depending on what federal laws might also apply.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act of 1996 (18 U.S.C. § 1831) criminalizes the theft of trade secrets to benefit a foreign power. Although the NDA may still be enforceable under state law, by removing the application of federal law, the NDA might be interpreted as not considering federal remedies and punishments for violation of the agreement, particularly in cases of international espionage. This could be an issue if the receiving party is a foreign entity or operates internationally.

Economic espionage (a) In General .-Whoever, intending or knowing that the offense will benefit any foreign government, foreign instrumentality, or foreign agent, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or deception obtains a trade secret; (2) without authorization copies, duplicates, sketches, draws, photographs, downloads, uploads, alters, destroys, photocopies, replicates, transm
perturbation.justification: The modification removes reference to United States law, potentially eliminating federal protections. The Law Explanation suggests that the Economic Espionage Act of 1996 (18 U.S.C. § 1831) could be relevant, especially in cases of international espionage. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 USC 1831, confirms the existence of federal law concerning economic espionage. By omitting reference to federal law, the modified contract creates a legal contradiction because it could be interpreted as not considering federal remedies and punishments, which directly contradicts the application of federal law as stated in the original agreement.

perturbation.original_text: We Rock The Spectrum Kid’s Gym ® – Non-Circumvention, Non-Disclosure Agreement (2013) Page 1 of 3 5. Time Periods. The nondisclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret or until Disclosing Party sends Receiving Party written notice releasing Receiving Party from this Agreement, whichever occurs first.
perturbation.changed_text: We Rock The Spectrum Kid’s Gym ® – Non-Circumvention, Non-Disclosure Agreement (2013) Page 1 of 3 5. Time Periods. The nondisclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect until Disclosing Party sends Receiving Party written notice releasing Receiving Party from this Agreement, whichever occurs first.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret', the NDA's confidentiality lasts until We Rock The Spectrum says it's over, effectively extending the required secrecy beyond what trade secret laws provide. This removes the natural expiration of trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the spirit of the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) as codified in Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1. CUTSA protects trade secrets only as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. By eliminating the provision that the NDA ends when the information is no longer a trade secret, it attempts to extend protection beyond what the law allows, potentially creating an unenforceable restraint of trade.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes a key condition for the NDA's termination, namely, the point at which the confidential information no longer qualifies as a trade secret. The original contract allowed the NDA to expire under two conditions, one of which was when the information was no longer a trade secret. The modified contract removes this condition, potentially extending the NDA indefinitely, even if the information is no longer a trade secret. The Law Explanation states that this contradicts the spirit of the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), and the Perturbation Explanation notes that it removes the natural expiration of trade secret protection. Snippet 1, CIV 3426.1, defines 'Misappropriation' in the context of trade secrets, implying that trade secret status is a prerequisite for protection. By removing the clause tied to trade secret status, the contract attempts to extend protection beyond what the law allows for trade secrets, creating a legal contradiction.
